UX Case Study SaaS CRM Redesign 2024 · Figma · Solo Designer

ActivProperties
CRM

Redesigning a broken real estate CRM into a clear, scalable, demo-ready SaaS product — for admins and agents simultaneously.

100+Screens Delivered
2User Roles
3–4wDesign Sprint
FigmaPrimary Tool
Overview

What is ActivProperties?

ActivProperties is a real estate CRM SaaS platform built for two types of users: Admins who oversee leads, properties, deals, and team activity at scale — and Agents who manage their own leads, listings, and deals day to day.

As the business grew, the existing interface started failing both groups. It was functionally complete but hard to use — and not impressive enough to demo to potential enterprise clients. The goal of this redesign was to fix both problems simultaneously.

🎯
My Role
End-to-end UX & UI — sole product designer from discovery through dev-ready Figma handoff.
👥
Worked With
Stakeholders & dev team. Close collaboration with business owner and engineering throughout.
🖥️
Users Designed For
Admins + Agents — two distinct roles, one unified platform, role-appropriate experiences for each.
📦
Deliverables
100+ hi-fi screens, component library, spacing system, and annotated Figma handoff.
Design Process

Six phases. One sprint.

01
DiscoveryStakeholder input & UI audit
02
ResearchCompetitor analysis
03
IA & StructureSitemap & nav
04
WireframesMid-fi layouts
05
UI Design100+ hi-fi screens
06
HandoffDev-ready Figma
Phase 1 — Discovery

Understanding the users.

Before identifying problems, I needed to understand who was actually using the platform and what they were trying to accomplish. Through stakeholder conversations and analysis of the existing system, two distinct user types emerged — each with different responsibilities, goals, and frustrations.

O
Omar Al Rashidi
Operations Admin · 38 yrs · Dubai
Admin User

Oversees the full pipeline — properties, leads, agents, and deals. Responsible for performance reporting and ensuring the business runs efficiently.

Goals:

  • See the full health of the business at a glance
  • Track lead and deal progress without chasing agents
  • Review and approve deals before they close
  • Demo the platform confidently to new clients

Frustrations with old UI:

  • No clear dashboard — had to dig through menus
  • Lead and deal status required cross-checking multiple screens
  • UI too outdated for client-facing demos
"I spend half my morning just trying to figure out where things stand. I shouldn't need to do that."
S
Sara Malik
Real Estate Agent · 27 yrs · Abu Dhabi
Agent User

Works directly with leads, matches them to properties, and closes deals. Spends most of her day in the leads and properties modules.

Goals:

  • Quickly find and contact her assigned leads
  • Match leads to the right properties efficiently
  • Track where each lead is in the pipeline
  • See her own performance and commission earnings

Frustrations with old UI:

  • Saw admin-only data not relevant to her work
  • Property matching was manual and time-consuming
  • No clear view of her own deal pipeline and commissions
"I just want to see my leads, match them to properties, and close. Everything else gets in the way."
Phase 1 — Problem Definition

What was going wrong.

With both user types in mind, I mapped the key pain points. Some problems affected both admins and agents — others were specific to each role.

Admin Pain Points
  • No at-a-glance pipeline health — KPIs were buried
  • Lead and deal progress required manual cross-screen tracking
  • Dense table layouts with no hierarchy or priority signals
  • UI too rough to use in client-facing demos
  • Agent performance invisible without drilling into reports
Agent Pain Points
  • Saw admin-level data irrelevant to daily work
  • No clear view of personal pipeline or commission status
  • Property matching required too many manual steps
  • Deal submission process was unclear and unintuitive
  • Activity tracking was disconnected from lead profiles
Core Insight

The system treated admins and agents as the same user — giving everyone the same dense interface. This meant admins were overwhelmed by operational clutter, and agents were distracted by data that wasn't relevant to their work. The redesign needed to create two distinct, role-appropriate experiences within a single platform.

Phase 2 — Research

Competitive analysis.

With no formal user research available, I studied three leading real estate platforms in the UAE market — Bayut, Dubizzle, and Property Finder — to identify the UX patterns and design standards users already expect.

Feature / PatternBayutDubizzleProperty Finder
KPI-first dashboard✓ Strong~ Basic✓ Strong
Role-based views✓ Yes✗ No✓ Yes
Card-based property layout✓ Yes✓ Yes✓ Yes
Visual lead stage tracking~ Partial✗ Weak✓ Clear
Agent performance dashboard✓ Yes✗ No✓ Yes
Avoids dense table-only views✓ Yes~ Mixed✓ Yes
Lead-to-property matching flow~ Partial✗ No✓ Yes

Every leading platform avoided dense table-only layouts, surfaced KPIs prominently, and provided role-specific views. These weren't differentiators — they were industry standards. ActivProperties needed to reach this baseline first.

Phase 3 — Information Architecture

Restructuring the navigation.

The old navigation was built around features — every module had equal weight with no hierarchy. I restructured it to be task-oriented and role-aware.

❌ Single navigation — admins and agents see identical screens
→
✅ Role-based navigation — each user sees only what's relevant
❌ No clear entry point — all features at equal visual priority
→
✅ Dashboard first — KPI summary cards as the entry point every session
❌ Lead info scattered across disconnected screens
→
✅ Unified lead profile — property matches, activity log, discussion in one view
❌ Deal submission unclear — no stage visibility or progress tracking
→
✅ Deals module with explicit stage tracking, review flow, and commission breakdown
❌ Agent performance invisible to admins without manual reporting
→
✅ Agent detail pages with performance charts, deal history, and commission overview
Phase 5 — UI Design

Key screens & design decisions.

Rather than designing screen by screen, I first built a design system — typography (Nunito Sans), a two-color brand palette (purple #6840ED + amber #F1A027), and a component library of reusable cards, tables, badges, and form elements. This gave the 100+ screens a consistent, professional foundation.

Below are key screens from the actual Figma deliverable — with the thinking behind each design decision.

Screen 01Admin Dashboard — The Command Centre

The admin sees the entire business at a glance: total leads (1,100), deals initiated, deals closed, average deal value, listing breakdowns by type, channel performance, and property status distribution. The KPI-first layout means admins never have to hunt for pipeline health — it's the first thing they see.

activproperties.app/dashboard
KPI cards surface total leads, deals, revenue at a glance Listings split by Sale / Rent / Lease with mini charts Status metrics donut + channel performance bar chart
Screen 02Create Property — Multi-Step Flow

Property creation is broken into 5 clear steps: Project & Property Basics → Media & Amenities → Pricing & Payment → Publishing & Registry → Documents. The progress stepper makes it impossible to lose your place. Media upload supports bulk image upload with a drag-and-drop zone and a paginated preview grid — a major improvement on the old single-file upload.

activproperties.app/properties/create/media
5-step progress stepper with clear current state Drag-and-drop bulk image upload, 20MB limit Paginated image preview grid with cover selection
Screen 03Properties Grid — Card-Based Listing View

Properties are displayed as cards — not tables. Each card shows the property photo, status badge (Pending/Approved/Draft/Published/Closed/Archived), listing type (Sale/Rent/Lease), active lead count, price, location, date, area, and quick-action icons. Agents can switch between My Properties and All Properties. Filtering by assigned agent, status, and sort order is all accessible from the top bar.

activproperties.app/properties
Card-based layout replaces dense table view Status + listing type badges on every card Active lead count surfaced without drilling in Grid / List / Map view toggle
Screen 04Deal Review — Admin Commission Workflow

The admin deal review screen consolidates everything needed to approve or reject a deal: deal amount (AED 5M), total receipts including VAT, and a tabbed view for Commission Details, Deal Details, Property Overview, and Activity Log. Commission fields are editable inline. The Approve / Reject / Request for Change actions are prominent in the top bar — no ambiguity about what the admin needs to do.

activproperties.app/deals/45821/review
Deal amount hero with status badge Commission Details tab with editable fields Clear Approve / Reject / Request Change actions
Screen 05Deal Details — Rejected Deal View

When a deal is rejected, the status is clearly shown as "Rejected" in the deal banner. The Deal Details tab shows the deal basics (purpose and type), key party information for both buyer and seller with contact details, and a documents section for sale deeds and related files. The full transparency of both sides of the transaction reduces back-and-forth between agents and admin.

activproperties.app/deals/45821/details
Rejected status clearly flagged in deal hero banner Buyer + seller contact details in one view Documents section with download capability
Screen 06Agent Details — Performance Overview

Admin can view any agent's full performance profile: contact info, total deals (12), recent activity, and four KPI cards — Total Deals Closed, Average Deal Value (340K), Total Commission Earned, and Conversion Rate (138K). Three charts give a complete picture: Monthly Performance (commission trend), Deal Distribution (closed/lost/negotiation), and Assigned Properties vs Deals Closed.

activproperties.app/agents/ali-khan
Agent profile with 4 KPI overview cards Commission trend + deal distribution charts Properties assigned vs deals closed comparison
Screen 07Lead Profile — Unified Lead View

The unified lead profile consolidates everything about a lead in one URL: contact information, property interest (2BR Apartment at Sobha Hartland, budget AED 160K–200K), lead source (Property Finder), documents, and the agent it's assigned to (Sara Ali). Below the fold: Property Matches (25 results), Shortlisted Properties, Activity Log, and Discussion Forum — all in tabs. The 95%/89%/98% match scores on property cards eliminate the guesswork of manual matching.

activproperties.app/leads/omar-siddiq
All lead info in one view — no cross-screen tracking Property match scores (95%/89%/98%) surface best fits Property Matches / Shortlisted / Activity Log / Discussion tabs Documents section for Emirates ID and client files
Screen 08Agent Deal Review — Commission Transparency

The agent-facing deal review screen is a simplified view of the same deal — showing only what the agent needs: deal basics, key party info (seller: Sri Priya), and a clear commission breakdown showing buyer agent share, seller agent share, and total estimated commission (25,000 AED at 4%). Agents can't approve or reject — their action is "Revoke Deal." The simplified tab structure (Deal Details & Commission, Property Overview, Activity Log) removes admin noise entirely.

activproperties.app/agent/deals/45821
Agent-only view — no admin noise Commission breakdown fully transparent Simplified tab set vs admin's 4-tab view Revoke Deal as the sole agent action
Outcomes

What changed.

100+
Hi-fi screens delivered
2
Distinct role experiences
1
Solo designer end-to-end
Dev
Ready Figma handoff

"Working with Ganesh was like when scissors glide through wrapping paper — seamless, effortless, and exactly right."

Reflections

What I learned.

The biggest lesson: role separation is a design problem before it's a development problem. When a system treats every user as generic, it fails everyone. Committing to two distinct experiences — even within the same UI shell — made clarity follow naturally for both user types.

Next steps: usability testing with real agents to surface edge cases in the property matching flow, and validating the commission dashboard with Sara-type users — data-heavy and emotionally significant territory where small friction has outsized impact.
